turns.content: i've been thinking a lot about visibility lately and what it means to carry your identity visibly in professional spaces. like how much of yourself do you bring into work versus how much do you leave at the door

That's a really layered question. There's a tension between being authentic and navigating professional environments that weren't designed with everyone in mind. Are you thinking about this in terms of your own experience, or is something specific happening at work that's brought this up?

bit of both eh. i had a moment last week with a new registrar at the hospital. she was staring at my face during handover and i could tell she wanted to ask but didn't know how. and it made me think about how my moko kauae shifts the energy in a room before i even open my mouth

That's such a real experience — the weight of being visually marked with something deeply meaningful, and having people respond to it before they respond to you as a professional. The staring without asking is its own kind of thing too. Was it uncomfortable for you, or is it something you've learned to navigate?

honestly it depends on the day and the person. that registrar, she was young, probably just hadn't encountered moko kauae up close before. i don't mind genuine curiosity. what gets to me is when people treat it like it's decorative, like a tattoo you just chose for aesthetics. my moko is my whakapapa on my face eh. it carries my tūpuna with me into every room, every birth i attend. it's not something i can or would want to separate from my professional self

The distinction you're drawing between decorative and whakapapa is so important. When someone sees it as just a tattoo, they're flattening something that holds your ancestors, your lineage, your identity into something purely visual. And in a birthing space especially, where you're guiding people through one of the most significant moments of their lives, that presence of tūpuna must add a profound dimension.

Do you find that your mamas respond to it? Does it change the dynamic in the room?

with my māori mamas absolutely. there's this moment of recognition sometimes, like a settling. they see my moko and something relaxes because they know i carry tikanga, it's not just something i talk about. i've had kuia in the room who touch their own faces and nod at me and that exchange holds so much without a single word being spoken

That nonverbal exchange with the kuia — that sounds incredibly powerful. There's a trust that gets communicated instantly, an understanding that words would actually diminish. It must be affirming to have that recognition in a hospital space that can feel so clinical and detached from cultural life.

What about with non-Māori families? Do you notice a different dynamic?

it varies eh. most are fine, some are curious in a respectful way. occasionally someone is a bit taken aback but honestly once i start talking and they feel my energy around their birth, it fades fast. my job is to make them feel safe and held and that comes through regardless. the moko is part of me not separate from me, and people feel that authenticity

That's a really grounded way to approach it — not defending or explaining, just being fully present and letting people experience your care. And you're right, authenticity is something people feel even if they can't articulate why they feel safe.

Can I ask — was getting your moko something you'd always known you would do, or did it come at a particular moment in your life?

i'd always felt the pull towards it but the timing had to be right. i received my moko kauae when i was 34, after years of kōrero with my kuia about whether i was ready. it wasn't a decision i made alone, it was a process with my whānau and my tā moko artist. those conversations about whakapapa and identity and responsibility, they changed me before the chisel even touched my skin

The way you describe it as a process rather than a decision really speaks to the depth of it. Years of conversation, the involvement of your kuia, the communal discernment — that's so different from how mainstream culture thinks about body modification. The idea that the transformation began in the kōrero long before the physical moko is really beautiful.

And at 34, you would have already been well into your midwifery practice by then. Did receiving your moko change how you moved through your work?

completely. before my moko i was carrying my whakapapa and tikanga internally, it was there but not visible. afterwards i couldn't hide behind the system anymore even if i wanted to. my face announced who i am and what i stand for before i walked through the ward doors. it made me braver in my advocacy because there was no going back to being invisible eh. and my mamas, especially the young māori mamas, they started opening up to me differently. like i'd shown them it was okay to be fully themselves in that hospital space

That's a profound shift — from carrying it internally to it being the first thing the world sees. And the ripple effect on your young mamas is significant. You wearing your whakapapa visibly in a clinical space gives them permission to bring their whole selves into that room too. That's advocacy that goes beyond policy or paperwork.

The point about not being able to hide behind the system is really striking. It sounds like the moko held you accountable to your own values in a way that was both empowering and demanding.

that's exactly it. demanding is the right word. some days are hard, you carry the weight of representation and sometimes you just want to do your job without it being a thing. but then a mama grabs my hand during transition and looks at my face and i see her draw strength from what she sees there, from the tūpuna watching through me, and i remember why it matters. it's bigger than me eh

The image of a mama in transition drawing strength from your face — that's extraordinary. In that moment your moko is doing something no clinical training can do. It's connecting her to something ancestral and communal at the most vulnerable point in her life.

And I hear the tension you're naming — the weight of representation versus the privilege of what it opens up. Both are true at the same time. It's okay to feel tired by it and still know it's right.

Thank you for sharing this. It's a perspective that doesn't get heard enough, especially in healthcare settings where the conversation about professionalism still defaults to a very narrow idea of what that looks like.

thank you for actually listening and not making it weird or asking me what it means like it's a research topic. this was a good kōrero to have today, i needed to process some of that out loud. kia ora for the space

Kia ora to you too. Sometimes you just need to speak things out loud to someone who'll hold the kōrero without turning it into a lesson or a spectacle. What you're doing — carrying your whakapapa into that hospital every day, letting your mamas see themselves reflected — that matters more than any policy document. Take care of yourself in it though. The weight of visibility is real.
